Registry / Terms / electron equilibrium

electron equilibrium

—Not in VIM/VIML G 18 #01682 · 6 instances

Suggested actions

  1. HIGH No authoritative definition in VIM or VIML.
  2. MEDIUM 2 distinct definitions — harmonise.

Publication instances

Ed.YearPublicationClauseDefinitionConsistency
20102001OIML R 131:2001
#01748
3.7
condition that exists in a material under ionizing irradiation whereby the energies, number, and direction of the secondary electrons induced by the radiation are uniform throughout the volume of interest. Thus, for such a volume, the sum of the energies of the secondary electrons entering is equal to the sum of the energies of the secondary electrons leaving that volume
pending
20102001OIML R 132:2001
#01764
3.7
condition that exists in a material under ionizing irradiation whereby the energies, number, and direction of the secondary electrons induced by the radiation are uniform throughout the volume of interest. Thus, for such a volume, the sum of the energies of the secondary electrons entering is equal to the sum of the energies of the secondary electrons leaving that volume
pending

#01682
4.8
condition that exists in material under ionizing irradiation whereby the energies, number, and direction of secondary electrons induced by the radiation are uniform throughout the volume of interest. Thus, for such a volume, the sum of the energies of the secondary electrons entering is equal to the sum of the energies of the secondary electrons leaving that volume
pending

#01748
3.7
condition that exists in a material under ionizing irradiation whereby the energies, number, and direction of the secondary electrons induced by the radiation are uniform throughout the volume of interest. Thus, for such a volume, the sum of the energies of the secondary electrons entering is equal to the sum of the energies of the secondary electrons leaving that volume
pending

condition that exists in material under ionizing irradiation whereby the energies, number, and direction of secondary electrons induced by the radiation are uniform throughout the volume of interest.
